title: F1CC 2026: Every Team, Every Driver — The Complete Season Guide
meta: Published: 9 February 2026 | By Marcus “Stats” Chen, Data & Performance Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Quarter Kevu arrives in Melbourne as the reigning F1CC World Champion.
body:
Melbourne is four days away. The paddock has assembled, pre‑season testing has spat out 3,042 laps of telemetry, and every conversation that’s been simmering for three off‑season months is about to be decided by the stopwatch. Here is everything the numbers tell us about the grid that will take to Albert Park on Sunday — every team, every driver, and the 14‑driver F2 academy web that ties this year’s feeder series straight to the sharp end.

Ferrari — Eetu Väisänen & Afonso Cachinho

Power unit: Ferrari

Ferrari enter 2026 with a 165‑point driver (Väisänen, P7 in 2025) and a seat that produced 226 points last season before Liam Dela Cruz walked away. That’s a 61‑point gap the team must somehow fill. Afonso Cachinho arrives from Haas having scored 121 points in 2025 — respectable, yes, but only 53.5% of Dela Cruz’s output. The personal thread is neat: when Väisänen got the Ferrari call mid‑2024, Cachinho backfilled his Haas seat for the remainder of that year. Now they share a garage. Team Principal Randy Belacik — who scored 14 points in his own final, truncated 2025 campaign before stepping out of the cockpit — has set the bar publicly. Ferrari’s F2 pipeline runs through Kanata Rivington at Hitech and Marcos Olmedo at Campos, two juniors who might suddenly find themselves under a brighter spotlight if that 61‑point deficit isn’t closed.

1 Dela Cruz’s 2025 average: 7.53 points per race. Cachinho’s: 4.03. Ferrari need a 3.5‑point‑per‑race improvement from the second seat just to tread water.

Red Bull — Jason Roy & Philippe Verhaeren

Power unit: Ford

One significant winter move, and it’s a gamble. Jason Roy steps across from VCARB with 148 points in 2025, heading to a Red Bull team that has not produced a drivers’ champion since Randy Belacik in 2024 — and that champion is now running a rival squad. The numbers of the past two seasons are awkward reading for the senior team: VCARB drivers have finished 1st and 3rd in the 2024 championship, and 1st again in 2025 (Kevu, 259 points). Red Bull’s best 2025 driver? Philippe Verhaeren with 130 points, a full 129 points off the champion’s tally. Roy believes he can extract what the mothership hasn’t. The data gives him a reasonable case: his 148 points from a junior car was 18 points more than Verhaeren’s output. Red Bull’s F2 depth is its saving grace — Jaromir Grunt, Takumi Aizawa, and Corentin Premeur, three academy drivers on this year’s F2 grid, more than any other constructor bar Mercedes.

VCARB — Quarter Kevu & Lukus Wright

Power unit: Ford

The statistical monster of the grid. Quarter Kevu, 259 points and the 2025 title. Lukus Wright, 264 points and the 2024 title from this very car. The VCARB machine has delivered back‑to‑back drivers’ championships with a combined 523 points from its two champions. Wright’s 163‑point 2025 at Red Bull was a 101‑point drop from his title year. Now he returns. Kevu plus Wright equals 422 points of proven championship‑winning output in this exact machinery. The burden of proof? It sits with everyone else. Racing Bulls also have Frederik Frid in F2 at Van Amersfoort Racing — one junior, but when your senior seats are occupied by two champions, you don’t need many.

McLaren — Zhao Lei & Joshua Nathanial

Power unit: Mercedes

Stability, quantified. Zhao Lei posted 168 points in 2025; Joshua Nathanial, 158 points. Combined 326 points — good for P5 in the constructors’ — but the trajectory is the story. Nathanial averaged 6.07 points per race over the final 14 rounds after a slow first half, while Lei’s reliability in mixed conditions yielded 12 top‑10 qualifying slots. No driver market splashes here, and in a paddock churning with change, that continuity is its own signal. McLaren’s F2 academy operates through three drivers: Ryan Melon (ART), Colin Hongnoi (Rodin), and Jacob Vavro (AIX). That’s three potential internal pressures should either senior seat wobble.

Mercedes — Wesley Vandesteene & Maikel Zwart

Power unit: Mercedes

The headline: Maikel Zwart’s 187 points from Williams in 2025 — P4 in the championship — moving to the works team. That 187‑point haul was 14 points more than the Mercedes duo combined in 2025 (Vandesteene’s 34 points in a partial campaign, plus Ignacio Urreta’s 31 points before leaving, and others). Zwart is a proven race winner in a faster car; he took 4 podiums and a victory from a car that finished P4 in the constructors’. Vandesteene, with only 34 points in a partial season, arrives as the unknown. Team Principal CMDesigns has assembled a pairing with a combined 221 points of 2025 output — but 84.6% of that came from one side of the garage. Mercedes’ F2 academy is the largest: four drivers — Korv Batt and Andrew Price at Prema, Kodiak Callahan at DAMS, Daniel Park and Alex Thompson at Trident.

Williams — Eren Aygen & Lucas Koopman

Power unit: Mercedes

The bluntest statistic in the paddock: Williams scored 201 points through Jack TEF in 2025, P3 in the championship. That driver is now team principal. In his place, Eren Aygen arrives from Mercedes with 186 points from a partial season — impressive, but a 15‑point step down from the driver he replaces. Lucas Koopman? Zero F1CC race starts. The combined 2025 output of the new pairing is 186 points, which is 7.5% less than what TEF alone delivered. Williams’ internal gap from 2025 to 2026 is the widest of any team retaining constructor status. The F2 pipeline runs through Hamish Porter at Van Amersfoort — one junior who might need to accelerate his timeline.

Aston Martin — Sarah Springfield & Sam Fakt

Power unit: Honda

Continuity in the cockpit: Sarah Springfield’s 92 points and Sam Fakt’s 72 points give a combined 164 points from 2025, exactly matching their constructors’ tally (P8). The major variable is underneath the bodywork — out goes the Mercedes PU, in comes a brand‑new Honda unit, a switch that brings zero carry‑over data. Fakt’s 6 retirements in 2025, most from his own driving, are the elephant in the room: he averaged 2.4 points per finish but threw away an estimated 28 points from DNFs from points‑scoring positions. Springfield, meanwhile, dragged 92 points from a car that often didn’t deserve them. Aston Martin has zero F2 academy affiliates on this year’s grid. Any driver problem this season means looking outside the family.

Alpine — Michiii MaraDöner & Julian Kondra

Power unit: Ford

The least experienced lineup on the grid, by the numbers: MaraDöner’s 43 points in a debut 2025 and Kondra’s partial past appearances without a full‑season benchmark. Alpine’s total driver output in 2025 was 117 points (P10 in constructors’). Nick Beier, who contributed 58 points — 49.6% of the team’s total — departs. That’s a significant loss measured in percentage contribution. The switch to the Ford‑badged Red Bull power unit is a bet on closing the 1.2‑second‑per‑lap deficit (estimated from 2025 season‑average lap‑time deltas to the front) that Alpine suffered. Yann Asdelian runs in F2 with DAMS, the sole academy driver feeding the project. The season will test whether 43 points plus an unknown equals more than the 58 points they let go.

Audi — Robin Keisner & Richard Hicks

Power unit: Audi

The rebrand from Kick Sauber is complete. The 2025 points baseline: 136 points from Keisner (67) and Duarte Sardinha (69), placing the team P9. Sardinha’s 69 points — 50.7% of the team’s output — are gone. Richard Hicks debuts with zero F1CC points. Keisner’s 67 points and flashes of late‑season qualifying speed (top‑10 grid slots in 3 of the final 6 rounds) offer a thread to pull. Audi’s new in‑house PU adds a layer of engineering demand that no other team faces: a brand‑new car and a brand‑new engine simultaneously. The F2 academy is the widest signal of intent — three drivers: Charles Mounir and Brock Lee at MP Motorsport, Marek Dubišar at Invicta Racing. That’s three junior cards for a team that might need one sooner rather than later if Hicks’s learning curve proves steep.

Haas — Riley Swansson & George Konstantaras

Power unit: Ferrari

Haas lose Cachinho’s 121 points (65.4% of their 185‑point 2025 constructors’ tally) to Ferrari. Retained: Riley Swansson, 64 points, a known quantity who posted 4 points finishes from the bottom quartile of the grid. New arrival George Konstantaras steps up with zero F1CC points. The combined 2025 output of the new pairing is 64 points — that’s a 65.4% loss of proven scoring. Tim Mittens runs in F2 with Hitech, the sole academy connection. Haas’s 2026 will be defined by how quickly Konstantaras can replace Cachinho’s point‑per‑race average of 4.03.

F2: The Next Generation

The 2026 F2 grid carries 14 drivers under direct F1CC academy contracts, the most in series history. Mercedes fields 4, Red Bull 3, Audi 3, McLaren 3, Ferrari 2, and every other constructor at least 1. Jaromir Grunt (Red Bull Academy) tops the pre‑season anticipation charts after an off‑season testing programme that saw him top 4 of 6 test sessions. Haas Academy’s Tim Mittens and Alpine’s Yann Asdelian arrive with realistic podium ambitions from round one. The density of F1‑backed talent means 57% of the F2 grid is directly tied to an F1CC seat decision. When the 2026 silly season kicks off, the data will already be flowing from these juniors.

The Championship Picture

Quarter Kevu’s 259 points and the VCARB’s 443‑point constructors’ title form the statistical baseline. No team in the last two years has beaten VCARB to the drivers’ crown. The chasing pack’s best: Maikel Zwart (187), Jason Roy (148 at VCARB, now Red Bull), Eetu Väisänen (165), Zhao Lei (168). The gap from the champion to the best non‑VCARB driver in 2025 was 72 points. In 2024, the gap from champion Lukus Wright (264) to non‑VCARB runner‑up Belacik (238) was 26 points. The trend is widening.

Driver2025 Points2025 RankCurrent Team
Quarter Kevu2591VCARB
Maikel Zwart1874Mercedes
Eren Aygen1865Williams
Zhao Lei1686McLaren
Eetu Väisänen1657Ferrari
Jason Roy14810Red Bull

Melbourne will not decide this championship. But it will tell us, inside 58 laps of racing, which of these numbers still hold — and which pre‑season projections need revising before the paddock even thinks about Shanghai. The 2026 season begins in four days.

[More data to follow]
